---
title: "4.17 Recursive Searching and Sorting"
subtitle: "Data Collections"
format:
  clean-revealjs:
    slide-number: true
    css: unit4-shared.css
lang: zh-CN
---

## Course Directory

### Return to the course outline

[← Back to AP CSA / 返回课程目录](../../index.html)

## Recursive Searching and Sorting

### Recursion now applies to two algorithms students already know iteratively

This topic connects earlier lessons:

::: {.tight-list}
- binary search from `4.14`
- sorting from `4.15`
- recursion from `4.16`
:::

## Recursive Binary Search

### Keep halving a sorted range until the target is found or no range remains

Retained algorithm story:

::: {.tight-list}
- binary search still requires <span class="mark">sorted data</span>
- check the middle element first
- recurse into the left half or the right half
- stop when the middle matches or when the active range is empty
:::

## Base Cases and Recursive Calls

### A recursive binary search needs two stopping conditions

```java
if (target == array[middle]) { return middle; }
if (end < start) { return -1; }
```

Retained reasoning:

::: {.tight-list}
- one base case handles <span class="term">found</span>
- one base case handles <span class="term">not found</span>
- the recursive calls change `start` or `end` to eliminate half the values
:::

## Recursive Binary Search on ArrayLists

### The same logic works with method-based access

Retained transfer:

::: {.tight-list}
- arrays use `array[middle]`
- `ArrayList`s use `list.get(middle)`
- the search logic is otherwise the same
:::

## Merge Sort

### Merge sort is a divide-and-conquer recursive sorting algorithm

Retained algorithm story:

::: {.tight-list}
- split the array into smaller subarrays
- recurse until each subarray has one element
- merge the sorted pieces back together
- merge sort is typically faster than selection sort and usually faster than insertion sort
:::

## Recognizing Merge Sort

### The AP version has three named responsibilities

Retained structure:

::: {.tight-list}
- `mergeSort` sets up the helper data structure
- `mergeSortHelper` performs the recursive splitting
- `merge` combines two sorted subarrays back into order
:::

This topic is less about memorizing every line and more about recognizing the control flow.

## Tracing Split and Merge

### Write the split stages first, then the merge stages

Retained trace pattern for `{86, 3, 43, 5}`:

::: {.tight-list}
- split 1: `{ {86, 3}, {43, 5} }`
- split 2: `{ {{86}, {3}}, {{43}, {5}} }`
- merge 1: `{ {3, 86}, {5, 43} }`
- merge 2: `{3, 5, 43, 86}`
:::

This is the merge-sort counterpart to tracing recursive binary search ranges.

## Classroom Tasks

### Practice worth keeping

Retained classroom work for this topic:

::: {.tight-list}
- identify the base cases in recursive binary search
- trace the `start`, `end`, and `middle` values through recursive calls
- explain why sorted order is required for binary search
- describe the role of `mergeSort`, `mergeSortHelper`, and `merge`
- trace merge sort by writing split and merge stages
- <span class="term">4.17.3 Tracing Challenge: Recursive Search and Sort</span>
:::

## Classroom Check

### A strong answer should...

::: {.tight-list}
- explain how recursive binary search eliminates half of the active range
- identify both recursive binary search base cases
- explain why binary search requires sorted data
- describe merge sort as recursive splitting followed by merging
- trace either a recursive search range sequence or a merge-sort split/merge sequence
:::

## End

### Return to the course outline

[← Back to AP CSA / 返回课程目录](../../index.html)
